scale.summary: A 6-axis rubric (1-5 each, total 30) for evaluating short Japanese graded-reader stories. Designed for early learners (roughly N5-N4 vocabulary and grammar). Each story is rated on language literacy, learner accessibility, narrative coherence, reader engagement, emotional resonance, and economy/polish.

axes.label: Literacy / Japanese quality
axes.description: Grammatical accuracy, particle correctness, idiomatic phrasing, register consistency, and overall naturalness of the Japanese prose. Penalize obvious calques, mis-used particles, awkward phrasings, or register clashes.

anchors.1: Frequent grammar or particle errors; unnatural or unclear Japanese.
anchors.2: Several awkward constructions; mostly readable but not idiomatic.
anchors.3: Generally correct; a few clumsy spots or odd word choices.
anchors.4: Smooth, idiomatic, learner-appropriate Japanese with only minor stiffness.
anchors.5: Clean, natural, fully idiomatic prose; could appear in a published graded reader.

axes.label: Learner comprehensibility
axes.description: How easy it is for an early learner to follow the story, given the assumed N5/N4 vocabulary base. Considers sentence length, syntactic complexity, vocabulary load, and clarity of reference (who/what is doing what).

anchors.1: Hard to follow even with effort; reference and meaning often unclear.
anchors.2: Frequent ambiguity; learners need outside help in many spots.
anchors.3: Mostly followable but a few ambiguous or vocabulary-heavy lines.
anchors.4: Clear throughout; small effort needed for one or two lines.
anchors.5: Crystal clear; appropriate sentence length and vocabulary load for the level.

axes.label: Narrative coherence
axes.description: Whether the story has a discernible shape (situation -> development -> resolution / payoff), stays in one scene or emotional frame, and avoids unrelated tail additions or topic drift.

anchors.1: Disjointed list of sentences; no real arc; visible drift or tacked-on lines.
anchors.2: Loose collection with a partial shape; tail clearly drifts.
anchors.3: Recognisable shape; some scene or topic shifts feel abrupt.
anchors.4: Coherent arc, single frame, only minor padding or coda wandering.
anchors.5: Tight, single-frame story with clear progression and a satisfying close.

axes.label: Reader engagement / interest
axes.description: How compelling and memorable the piece is. Looks at imagery, scene-setting, hook, sense of curiosity, freshness of subject, and the presence of a turn or surprise.

anchors.1: Flat; nothing pulls the reader forward; generic situation.
anchors.2: Mildly pleasant but predictable; few specific images.
anchors.3: Some specific images or a small turn; holds interest.
anchors.4: Distinct imagery, mood, or hook; the reader wants to keep reading.
anchors.5: Memorable, vivid, and surprising; lingers after reading.

axes.label: Emotional resonance
axes.description: Depth and authenticity of feeling - warmth, melancholy, intimacy, longing, joy. Avoids sentimentality but rewards earned affect.

anchors.1: Emotionally inert; pure description with no felt texture.
anchors.2: Slight mood without real pull; generic feeling-words.
anchors.3: Clear mood; some lines land emotionally.
anchors.4: Strong, specific affect that feels earned by the scene.
anchors.5: Quietly powerful; a small emotional epiphany or vivid mood.

axes.label: Economy and polish
axes.description: Whether each sentence pulls weight. Penalizes filler, redundant restatement, late-added 'reinforcement tail' sentences (typical of automated drills), and visibly didactic constructions.

anchors.1: Heavy padding or visible drill tails; many lines do nothing.
anchors.3: Some padding, especially near the end, but core is tight.
anchors.4: Mostly tight; one or two slightly soft lines.
anchors.5: Every sentence earns its place; no padding, no drill residue.

scoring_notes: All stories were read in their bilingual JSON form from pipeline/inputs/.
Scores reflect the input source text, not any downstream rendering or audio.
Comprehensibility assumes the reader is roughly at JLPT N5 progressing toward N4.
Tail-drift (a cluster of unrelated sentences appended to a finished scene) is the single most common reason economy_polish drops below 4.

stories.notes: Compact, clean rain-and-tea mood piece; perfectly level-appropriate; nothing wasted.
Pleasant parallelism but the two-friends-feeling-good ending is a touch flat.
Tight breakfast scene; the closing apposition works as a clean coda.
List-like but coherent; final apposition closes it neatly.
Tightened: door+wind ending now flows; 'そして' jumps reduced to one.
Closing apposition now (cat, flowers, quiet window) — all in-frame.
More image-cluster than story, but the imagery is rich and tightly drawn.
Two-locations symmetry (park, then home) works; ending is calm but unsurprising.
Clear cat-bird-letter arc with a quiet emotional close; one of the best in the set.
Logic patched: dog now sleeps beside the chair, narrator on chair — no more conflict.
Past-tense letter scene; appositional final sentence works as a coda image.
Cat-watches-rain-and-watcher conceit holds; the trailing '小さい鳥がいます' feels tacked-on.
Quietly literary; the closing garden image is a touch loose but evocative.
Strong 痛い手紙 image; '〜ておきます' usage is slightly stiff but in-level.
Beautiful walk-and-return; child's song as 'night's music' lands well.
Pleasant picnic scene; the rain-arrival ending feels engineered for variety.
Warm bench scene; the moving '歩くとき' insight closes well.
Sad-then-happy turn works; the '友達は来ません' line is a sharp pivot.
Yesterday-child line now joins the bird-and-bread frame instead of jumping out of it.

stories.title_en: A Letter on a Cold Morning
stories.notes: Letter-writing scene with cold/warm contrast; '痛い手紙' is intriguing.
Revised close folds 'friend doesn't come' into the door image rather than dangling it.

stories.title_en: A Photo in the Old Book
stories.notes: Nostalgia-via-photograph works; the inserted '〜歩きませんか' is slightly didactic.
Tomorrow-tea invitation now precedes the tomorrow-sun line; less drill, more arc.
Spring/autumn contrast and arrival scene work; somewhat overstuffed.
Quietly devastating loneliness piece; 'でも、外は静かです' is a perfect ambivalent close.
Clean coming-home arc; the closing apposition lands.
Static tableau; well-formed but low forward motion.
Train-shop-clock now consolidated into one in-frame sentence pair.
Closing apposition now ends on 'good morning' instead of an unanchored '時計'.
Best-in-set: a full sad-walk arc with a perfect homeward close.
Phone-call format adds variety; small bookkeeping items (鍵も机に) feel inserted.
Father-call dialogue is warm; '寒い道を走ります' is an odd image swerve.
Strong memory-clock core; '町は静かです。人が来ます' coda wanders off.
Tail rewritten as the homeward turn (key, dog, rain-sound outside) — no grandmother insertion.
Mother-letter scene with bittersweet distance; town/customers line breaks frame.
Near-ideal arrival-by-the-stove miniature; final bread line is the only soft spot.
Lovely 'name from mountain and river' core; the trailing 兄/祖母 lines diffuse it.
Strong quiet-romance seed; 池/兄 closing lines stretch the frame.
Romantic rescue scene with vivid sensory close; 寒い朝/ストーブ tail is mildly extra.
Beautiful '言葉は小さい' centre; the last five lines cycle through unrelated images.
'言葉は小さいですから、大切です' is gold; the school/river/stove lines crowd the close.

stories.title_en: The Two Of Us, On the Road
stories.notes: Backstory now folded into 'the reply I won't tell my friend' — no abrupt aside.
Tomorrow-projection rebuilt as an in-frame meditation on brother, family, key.
Tail collapsed into a single past/present contrast (yesterday cold / spring warm).
Box-of-memories now closes on grandmother handing the box and showing the clock.
'春は何ですか。これは緑の光、温かい朝です' is genuinely poetic; minimal padding.
Question-driven elimination structure works beautifully; '弟のです' coda is light but tolerable.
Search-the-shop frame is lively; closing apposition lands.
Cozy invitation scene; the 鍵がなくて line breaks the warm tone right at the close.
Tail re-anchored to the grandmother-and-child waiting frame.
Entryway scene now part of the homecoming, not a separate snapshot.
Home-visit frame works; the brother-reads-letter line is an unrelated insertion.
Bookstore extension now flows: clock shown by the bookstore person, hat-and-shoes anchored at the entryway.
Past-tense regret piece is well-shaped; '鐘の音は時々静か' is slightly awkward phrasing.
Sister/kitchen/coat now form one sister-portrait inside the same memory.
Drill cluster rewritten as in-frame park-shop coda (hallway, teacher, bell).

stories.title_jp: 雨が降りますが、明るい朝。
stories.title_en: Rain Falls, But a Bright Morning.
stories.notes: Conjunctive 〜が drill is well-disguised; '一年は早いです' is a slightly odd insertion.
Walk-toward-the-bookstore desire is clear; closing apposition is overlong.
Drill tail collapsed into a single dream-bridge image and one closing apposition.
Tail folded into kitchen + bell + 'my number-one person is grandmother' before the time apposition.
Beautifully sustained interrogation between grandmother and narrator; 今週/今日 tail is the only soft spot.

stories.title_en: It's a Good Book, You Know.
stories.notes: Quiet bookstore intimacy; 〜よ register-shift is well-controlled and warm.
Village/parking/bell now one image; sentence on grandmother's kindness pulled inside the same frame.
Plain-form past drill mostly disguised as journal entries; 夢/村/山と川 trio is unrelated tail.
Tail collapsed: shop-bought map+newspaper, daydream, evening bridge — three lines instead of six.
Back-half drill cluster now a continuous bookstore-person dialogue with map/newspaper/cover folded in.
だから refrain ties memory, encounter, and feeling together; final three-image apposition is earned.

findings: Literacy is the strongest axis across the corpus (mean 4.87): the Japanese itself is consistently natural and level-appropriate.

Economy and polish is by far the weakest axis (mean 3.37): the most common defect is a tail cluster of 2-6 unrelated sentences appended to an otherwise complete scene, almost certainly a residue of automated drill or vocabulary reinforcement.
Narrative coherence (3.94) is depressed by the same tail-drift problem and by occasional mid-story topic insertions.

Engagement (4.04) and emotional resonance (4.15) are both reliably good: when the stories work, they work because of a single vivid image (a small key, a moonlit road, a same moon, a shared umbrella, a grandmother's clock).
The S-tier stories share three traits: a single emotional frame, a small turn or payoff, and no visible drill tail. Story 30 (秋の道) is the only perfect 30/30.

methodology_notes: Each story was read in full from its bilingual source in pipeline/inputs/.
Scores were assigned in a single pass using the rubric anchors in scale.axes.
Comprehensibility was rated against an assumed early N5-to-N4 reader.

Where a story has a strong core but a clearly tacked-on tail, narrative_coherence and economy_polish are penalized while literacy and engagement remain high; this gap is the most diagnostic signal in the corpus.

changelog.description: Rewrote 23 stories with drill-tail or scene-drift defects. Each tail or off-frame insertion was either deleted, rewritten in-frame, or consolidated. Vocabulary footprint preserved: every word that depends on a story for first-occurrence, R1 reinforcement, or orphan avoidance was retained or relocated within the same story. After rewrites, all 156 pipeline tests pass except the pre-existing audio/sentence count check (Phase 8, deferred).
